data.title: Nvidia: The New Boeing In China
data.description:
In December, I discussed the then-upcoming approval of Nvidia Corporation's (NVDA) H200 chip exports to China. At the time, I pointed out that sales to China were highly uncertain and implementing higher sales expectations driven by increased sales to China would be premature. The latest news is that China is not accepting shipments of the advanced H200 chips. In this report, I discuss why that might be the case and how Nvidia is becoming the new Boeing (BA) in the trade turmoil.

The Revenue Sharing Agreement That Wasn't

Initially, it seemed as if Nvidia had reached an agreement to ship H200 sales to China; As part of a revenue sharing agreement, China would get access to the advanced H200 chip which was a step up from the dumbed down version of H20 developed for the Chinese market in exchange for the U.S. Government taking a 25% cut.

On the 14th of January, the outline of the mechanism was announced. H200 chips would not be directly shipped to China from the production location but first to the U.S. Upon entry in the U.S., a 25% tariff would be applied to be paid by the importing party in the U.S. After that, the H200 could be shipped to China.

A true revenue sharing agreement would reduce Nvidia’s revenues, as the customer would pay $25 if we hypothetically assume a sale of $100 worth of H200 chips at a cost of good of $40. It would imply margins of 46.7%. If Nvidia were to charge a higher price essentially passing through the tariff, it would pay 25% based on the $100 worth of revenues that is being imported to the U.S. before exporting it, but that is not a revenue item as the company would merely serve as the collecting agent and the passing through is settled outside of the P&L. As a result, the $25 is fully paid for by the customer. If Nvidia were to absorb the higher tariff, it would essentially have the $25 tariff as an expense, as gross margins would drop to 35%.

So, from an accounting perspective, these are all different margin scenarios. We also note that the current 25% levy whether passed through or absorbed by Nvidia is fundamentally different in timing. The revenue share would trigger once a sale occurs, while the pass-through and tariff absorption have the $25 fee applied upon entry into the U.S. Revenue sharing would seem like a perfect tool for President Trump with a business approach. However, it would effectively be a forced royalty or export tax, which would not only be bad for Nvidia’s top line but also be violating Article I, Section 9, Clause 5 Exports:

No Tax or Duty shall be laid on Articles exported from any State.

Whether Nvidia is passing through the levy that it pays on import is not known, but what is known is that what currently is being executed is not a revenue sharing agreement. Perhaps that provides a hurdle for China to accept H200 sales, but it is also possible that the country is maximizing leverage or stalling a chip that at this moment they do not need, also to increase leverage. While Nvidia does not bake in sales to China for the H200 in its guidance, the block by China is painful as Nvidia had upside for 1 million orders valued $30 billion.

Painful Parallels With Boeing

The fact that China now refuses a product cleared by the U.S. reminds me in some ways of the Boeing 737 MAX. During the trade war initiated during President Trump’s first term, China retaliated by imposing tariffs on U.S. airplanes. The country, though, carefully sculpted the weight limits such that it would actually not obstruct deliveries of the Boeing 737 MAX.

However, after the second crash of a MAX in 2019 the country was quick to ground the Boeing 737 MAX, and only ungrounded the MAX once demand forced the country to do so and paced fleet reactivations and new deliveries in line with demand. The Boeing 737 MAX had essentially become the playball of trade politics and while the situation with the H200 is different, we are seeing that yet again China is making a U.S. product the playball of trade politics. This is regulatory gatekeeping as leverage. In that regard, I also believe it is a good step that Nvidia may have slowed down H200 and even halted production:

Suppliers of parts for Nvidia’s H200 have paused production after Chinese customs officials blocked shipments of the newly approved artificial intelligence processors from entering China.

In 2025, Boeing deliveries to China were briefly grounded, resulting in a temporary increase in inventory. For the H200 chip, it is extremely important that Nvidia does not produce the chip in large quantities if the Chinese market does not open up soon as H200 is based on the older Hopper architecture while the current standard architecture for global sales is Blackwell and the Rubin/Vera Rubin is set to launch in late 2026. Continuing production would substantially increase inventory and the risk of write-offs, given the limited appeal of the H200 in other markets.

The Best Expectation Is To Not Expect Sales To China

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Setting a price target for Nvidia stock can be challenging. I believe a low-end price target would be $201.54 in line with the peer group and 2027 earnings in mind. The base case would be $243.53 based on 2026 earnings indicating 33% upside and more optimistically we would stick with a peer group valuation and shift our focus from FY27 to FY28 as the company is heading into FY27 and looking one year ahead from there we can pull FY28 into the estimates.

I previously also established a price target based on the average implied price targets for 2027 and that would now indicate a $303.20 price target with 65% upside. However, under the current circumstances, that is not a price target that, I believe, is achievable unless there will be positive news on sales to China. What I do like about the current valuation is that there is upside based on forecast earnings for FY27, regardless of whether the company or peer group EV/EBITDA multiple is used.

For Nvidia, we are actually seeing a high degree of stability in the growth. That may sound weird. However, we continue to see strong margins, cash conversion north of 70%, low CapEx against operating cash flow and quasi-linear growth expectations for free cash flow driving the cash position to $200 billion by 2028, no net debt and gross debt being extremely low against the EBITDA performance.

Conclusion: Nvidia Remains Attractive Even Without China

Approval of sales in China would be a catalyst for Nvidia share prices. However, like I concluded in my prior report there is a lot of uncertainty regarding H200 chip sales to China and my fear remains that the approval of deliveries to China has come little to late in the cycle and China is now in the position to make the H200 chips part of the trade tension in the same way it has done with the Boeing 737 MAX. That would be unfortunate for Nvidia, but we note that the stock now trades at discount to peers without sales expectations for China. Other semiconductor names have surged while Nvidia stock has lagged behind and that potentially provides some safer upside for Nvidia shares.

data.title: Suncor: Moving My Profit-Taking Target
data.description:
Investment thesis.

Given a number of fundamental oil market trends, coupled with broader stock market dynamics, I decided to shift my starting point for profit taking on my Suncor (SU) shares, which make up the largest stock position in my portfolio. i am also increasing my buy target.

From a supply/demand oil market perspective, 2025 ended with a convergence of factors that may have created a new, perhaps permanent all-time high for global crude oil supplies. US crude oil output increased by about 700,000 b/d from the start of 2025 to the end. At the same time, the OPEC+ group added about 2 mb/d in output last year, unwinding most of the real spare capacity available to its members. With the OPEC+ group arguably out of real spare capacity available to bring online, and the EIA forecasting an 800,000 b/d decline in US crude oil output by the end of 2027, we now seem to be in a sustained oil market tightening phase.

In the absence of a wildcard event, such as Venezuela deploying its massive reserves to duplicate the effect that the US shale boom had on the global oil markets between 2010 and 2025, the global oil market will most likely gradually and sustainably tighten from here until a demand destruction event brings it back into balance. Keeping this in mind, I intend to start buying Suncor shares incrementally on the way down, if its share price drops below $45. I intend to incrementally start taking profits if its share price rises above $55, while maintaining Suncor as one of my largest portfolio positions. In other words, I do not intend to fully liquidate my position.

My prior coverage of Suncor.

The last time I covered Suncor, I highlighted its solid Q3 financial results in the face of what we can consider to be a weak oil price environment. Given its strong share price performance and generous dividend, I see my position in this stock as the largest holding in my portfolio, as justified. I had the stock as a buy then, and I continue to have it as a buy now, even though it has briefly touched my $50/share price target at which I previously stated that I intend to start taking profits. My reasoning behind this move is that, aside from company-specific and oil market fundamentals, we should increasingly account for broader market fundamentals, where oil stocks are under-priced relative to the broader market.

A brief look at Suncor's company profile.

I covered Suncor's Q3 results in my last article, so I am not going to spend too much time focusing on it this time around. Its upstream production increased by 8% compared with the previous quarter, reaching a new record of 870,000 b/d. Net earnings improved from 1.13 billion Canadian dollars in Q2, to 1.62 billion. Revenues declined by 4% in the first nine months of 2025.

Within the context of declining oil prices, Suncor's financial performance held up decently. It sets the stage for its share price to surpass the all-time highs it reached in 2008 of $71/share, if I am correct about the potential for a sustained oil price rise starting in the second half of this year.

As the chart shows, not only did Suncor's share price advance significantly in the past year, even as oil prices declined over 20% for the period, but it also outperformed its oil major peers, all of which advanced in the past year. I should note that the returns represented in the chart above include the dividend effect.

Oil producers' stocks are defying oil price trends.

It can be argued that the reason why oil producers gained in share price in the past year, even as oil prices declined, is that the market is forward-looking and, as such, it is betting on higher oil prices in the future by positioning for it now. As I shall explain, I am one of the investors betting on higher oil prices in the future, based on the assumption that the oil market is set to start tightening from this point forward.

Another, less obvious reason for oil producers to rally is the fact that the broader stock markets are trading at rising valuation levels.

With stock markets trading at roughly double the P/E ratio they have historically, it is reasonable to expect oil producers to start trading at a higher P/E ratio as well. Suncor is currently trading at a P/E ratio of 16, whereas a year ago it was trading at a P/E ratio of about 10. It still has a lower P/E ratio compared to ExxonMobil (XOM), which is trading at a forward ratio of 19, and Chevron (CVX), at 23. Shell (SHEL) is trading at a forward P/E ratio of around 12, which is lower than Suncor's, but this is arguably due to its very poor upstream reserves situation.

The net effect of the market's rising P/E ratio on all sectors of the market is to lift all boats higher. The oil sector in particular may rise at a faster pace compared with the broader markets, because investors are increasingly looking for bargains within a historically overpriced market, while low oil prices make for a favorable entry point, if one assumes higher oil prices in the future. I foresee a slight convergence between oil producer P/E ratios and the market's overall P/E ratio, which itself seems to be poised to keep rising.

Global oil market balance to see steady tightening.

The world is currently experiencing a period of oil market surplus, with the actual volume up for institutional debate. OPEC sees a more or less balanced global market, while the EIA sees a sizable 2.5 mb/d surplus currently, mostly due to US output rising by about 700,000 b/d, and OPEC+ raising output by over 2 mb/d last year. The IEA sees a world that is currently drowning in crude oil, which will continue to be the case for the foreseeable future. All three institutions cannot be correct, since there is such a sizable gap between OPEC on one hand and the EIA & IEA on the other.

My personal view is that we might find reality somewhere in the middle, in between the two opposed institutional camps. In other words, there is most likely an oil output surplus on the global market, but it might not be as great as the likes of EIA are claiming. Furthermore, we most likely entered a period of sustained global oil market tightening at the start of this year.

The EIA is forecasting a drop of about 800,000 b/d in US oil output between the fourth quarter of 2025 and the fourth quarter of 2027. OPEC+ is credited with still having as much as 4-5 mb/d in spare capacity, which is pure fiction in my view. Interestingly, the EIA & IEA both failed to significantly reduce the spare capacity estimates for OPEC members, even though they brought a significant volume of spare capacity back into production last year.

As we can see, the EIA believes that OPEC currently has about 3.5 mb/d in spare capacity this year, after last year's increase in output, based on their latest monthly report.

In its January 2025 report it had OPEC spare capacity forecast at 4 mb/d for this year, after the organization added about 1.5 mb/d in production last year, and there are few indications that OPEC members managed to significantly add to their production capacity last year or that they will do so this year.

Regardless of how we look at this data, it seems that the EIA is reluctant to fully subtract OPEC's additions from its spare capacity estimates. Those estimates are historically inflated, with examples such as Saudi Arabia and other OPEC members caught in 2008 unable to meaningfully increase oil output from their claimed spare capacity, which led to the all-time high record in oil prices that year.

OPEC+ is unlikely to increase output this year or next. Any spare capacity that some members may still hold is most likely to be kept in reserve in the event of a significant global oil supply disruption, which would require them to demonstrate their ability to react. At the same time, US oil output growth that kept the world from stagnating oil production between 2010 and 2025 is now headed in reverse, with output set to steadily decline by about 100,000 b/d every quarter or so. Global oil demand is still increasing by about 1 million barrels per day (mb/d) every year. I see oil prices responding to this situation of market tightening in the second half of this year, when the supply/demand balance will look significantly different, assuming that oil demand continues to grow as expected.

Investment implications.

With institutions forecasting a world that is oversupplied with oil and will continue to be for the foreseeable future, and widespread acceptance of those forecasts, it may be difficult for many investors to see the value of Suncor from my vantage point. My view is that the temporary supply surplus created in 2025 is gradually dissipating, with demand growth on one end, and several countries set to see a decline in output on the other end, resulting in a relatively fast rebalancing of the global supply/demand equation, which will be followed by a swing into deepening supply deficits.

Within this context, Suncor is an ideal investment candidate within the oil sector, mostly because it has a comparatively superior upstream reserve situation.

It is currently increasing its upstream production. It reached a new all-time record high in Q3, with output expected to be higher in 2026 on the upper end of the forecast by about 2% compared with the upper end of the estimate for this year. It has a proven ability to profitably produce oil at current market prices.

With a higher P/E ratio due to its share price appreciating significantly in the past year, the dividend offered to investors buying shares at the current price level is no longer as generous, at about 3.5%, however, I think it is still worth buying this year on any significant dips, on expectations of its share price rising from here. I am looking to start buying only if it dips below $45/share, which is higher than my prior $40/share target I had in place a few months back. I am looking to take profits incrementally, if or when its share price rises above $55/share.

I might consider reducing my current sizable position in this stock if it rises above the all-time high reached in 2008, of over $70/share, but I will probably maintain a position in this stock regardless of how high its share price rises. My average cost basis for this stock is about $23/share, therefore, its current dividend expected to be $1.73/share this year is rather generous from my perspective.

Risks to my thesis.

As I pointed out in a recent article, the move by the Trump administration to replace the Maduro government in Venezuela may have the net effect of delaying a global peak in crude oil output by perhaps decades. This means that if all goes according to plan, oil prices may rise from current levels, but the oil market will mostly remain stable, with somewhat limited upside. In this event, Suncor's potential upside may be somewhat limited as well. Venezuelan oil output growth will require higher oil prices on a sustained basis from current levels; therefore, even if Venezuela increases oil output considerably, things are still somewhat positive for Suncor, since oil sands breakeven levels are lower than the price needed to extract Venezuela's oil, therefore a new price floor, at a higher level than the current one will form.

A demand destruction event, due to an unforeseen factor, as we saw during the COVID crisis, or perhaps due to a slowdown in the global economy, is always a persistent and potent risk factor for the oil industry. Oil is an inelastic good; therefore, it does not take much of a supply shortfall to make market prices spike, or much of a demand destruction event to have prices plunge. If we do see a demand destruction event, I intend to use it as a buying opportunity.

Conclusions.

Until recently, I mostly focused my buy thesis for Suncor on the obvious fundamentals that directly impact its financial performance, thus its share price. I mostly overlooked the likely effect that we can see from the overall market continuing to see a rise in P/E ratios, which inevitably leads to oil companies also seeing a rise in their P/E ratio. Since companies like Suncor are trading at a P/E ratio that is less than half compared with the broader markets, there is arguably more room for oil companies to see their stock price appreciate going forward than there is for the broader markets.

Aside from the broader market dynamics, the future performance of oil company stocks will depend on internal factors, as well as external oil market dynamics going forward. Since I last covered Suncor, the EIA came out with its first monthly report of the year, which for the first time includes forecasts for 2027. Based on that report, US crude oil output is set to decline by 800,000 b/d from Q4, 2025 levels, while global demand for oil will increase by about 2.4 mb/d this year and next.

These two factors alone amount to a market tightening by about 3.2 mb/d by the end of 2027. There are, of course, places like Canada, Guyana, or Argentina where we might see an increase in oil production, but there are also other places like Norway, which is set to see its output shrink significantly in coming years.

Suncor stands out as one of the best-positioned companies to benefit from a sustained tightening of the global oil markets, along with oil sands peers such as CNQ (CNQ). It has ample reserves that it can produce at reasonable breakeven costs, to support continued output growth, thus, continued improvement in its financial results for the longer term. Stronger financial results could potentially result in a further increase in its dividend down the line. For these reasons, I decided to raise my buy target price, as well as the minimum level at which I intend to start gradually taking profits. This is a company that is worth keeping in one's portfolio for the long haul, which is what I intend to do.

data.title: Sell Alert: 3 REITs Facing Likely Dividend Cuts
data.description:
Most REITs are in a good position today to keep growing their dividend payments:

  • Rents continue to grow steadily in most property sectors.
  • Dividend payout ratios are historically low.
  • REIT balance sheets are the strongest they have ever been.
  • This allows REITs to keep acquiring new properties to grow cash flows.
  • And finally, the Fed is gradually lowering interest rates.

As a result, it was recently reported that REITs are expected to grow their dividend by another 5% on average in 2026.

That's very attractive given that a 5% dividend yield, coupled with 5% dividend growth, should result in a 10% annual total return, assuming that the dividend yield remains intact.

And that's just the average. Lots of REITs are expected to do better than that.

But there is also a minority of REITs that are facing significant headwinds and are at high risk of cutting their dividends in 2026. This could then negatively affect their market sentiment and potentially lead to lower share prices.

That's going to be the focus of today's article. Be careful about these potential dividend disasters:

Clipper Realty (CLPR)

Many REIT investors are buying shares of Clipper Realty (CLPR) because it is the single highest-yielding REIT that owns apartment communities.

It is currently offering a 10% dividend yield, which, based on 2025 AFFO, appears to be safely covered with a 60% payout ratio.

Moreover, the REIT is enjoying steady, strong growth in its rents. It focuses primarily on affordable housing in Manhattan and Brooklyn, which are both severely undersupplied, resulting in near-100% occupancy rates and strong rent growth.

So what could go wrong here?

The 2025 payout ratio and rent growth do not tell the full story. The REIT owns two office buildings as well, which generate about a quarter of its rental income, and one of them recently became vacant.

This would be fine if the REIT had a strong balance sheet, but it doesn't. It is one of the most heavily leveraged REITs that I know of, with a debt-to-EBITDA of about 15x and short debt maturities.

They will likely need to heavily reinvest in that office building if they want to release it, and this will likely cost them their dividend.

I don't see how they avoid a cut. They pushed it as long as they could, but with their balance sheet already stretched, I think that they are now reaching their limit.

To be clear, this does not mean that CLPR is a bad investment. I actually own a small position, but this is a special situation, not an income investment. I actually hope that they fully suspend the dividend and focus on deleveraging eventually to close their massive gap to NAV, which could result in a doubling of the current share price.

Don't buy CLPR for the dividend. It is likely to be short-lived.

Gladstone Land (LAND)

Gladstone Land (LAND) is also attracting a lot of dividend investors for similar reasons. It is the highest-yielding farmland REIT out there. Its share price has dropped a lot even as the REIT kept growing its dividend in recent years, and as a result, it is now offering a historically high 5.6% dividend yield:

But once again, I think that the current dividend is unlikely to last.

The company has been facing headwinds from declining rents, and its balance sheet is heavily leveraged with nearly 70% leverage when including the preferred equity:

The current dividend is already poorly covered, and the coverage will likely only get worse over the coming years as more of its debt matures and needs to be refinanced at higher rates.

The management has sought to avoid cutting the dividend as it would ruin a long track record of steady dividend growth, but again, here, they have pushed it as long as they could, and I think that 2026 could be the year when they finally adjust the dividend.

Given that a lot of investors have been buying it as a "safe near 6% yielding farmland REIT", this cut could lead to quite a bit of downside.

I monitor the REIT closely and may invest after it has cut the dividend but will stay on the sidelines until then.

Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN)

Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN) cut its dividend in 2023 and in 2025, and I predicted both of these cuts in previous articles that you can read by clicking here and here.

Today, I am predicting that a third cut is just around the corner.

The REIT owns offices primarily in Philadelphia, and it has a large development pipeline, a lot of which is life science buildings.

Both property sectors are severely oversupplied, even as the REIT has a lot of leverage with an 8.8x debt-to-EBITDA, and its current dividend is not covered:

The fundamentals of the life science property sector greatly deteriorated in 2025 as a result of the policy chaos of the current administration, and this even forced Alexandria Real Estate (ARE), which had a much stronger BBB+ rated balance sheet and lower dividend payout ratio, to cut its dividend.

This puts great doubt over BDN's life science development pipeline, especially given how heavily leveraged it already is.

I think that a third dividend cut is just around the corner.

The valuation is low, but so is that of Alexandria. If you want to invest in this space, I would much rather invest in Alexandria, which owns better assets on average and has a much better balance sheet and long-term track record.

Closing Note

Our selection process is not bulletproof, and Alexandria is a great example of that, as we held a position in it already before it cut its dividend and crashed to much lower levels.

But overall, we have managed to avoid most dividend cuts at High Yield Landlord, and avoiding those losses has greatly put us ahead of our sector benchmarks.

data.title: Patience Continuing To Pay Off For Citizens Financial Group
data.description:
A better operating environment and consistent execution from management have brought a lot of benefits for investors who have had the patience to stick with Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (CFG) over the last couple of years. Better still, the bank looks well-placed to continue to generate above-average growth and further close its ROTCE gap with major peers, both of which should support further re-rating.

Citizens shares are up about 170% since my last update, handily outperforming not only regional banks as a group (up less than 80%) and closer peers like Fifth Third (FITB), KeyCorp (KEY), and M&T Bank (MTB).

I do have some concerns about asset sensitivity and increasing competition in areas like capital markets, payments, private banking, and middle market lending, but Citizens is doing well, and I don’t see any obvious near-term challenges apart from overall macro conditions. Although the shares aren’t so clearly undervalued now, there’s still enough undervaluation to make it a worthwhile holding.

Another Good Quarter

Citizens’ fourth quarter results weren’t exactly a blowout, but the market rewards consistency, and Citizens management continues to deliver that with a good run of earnings reports a few pennies ahead of expectations and keeping the company on track to mid-to-high-teens ROTCEs in a few years.

Fourth quarter revenue rose more than 8% year over year and almost 2% sequentially, beating expectations by a little less than 1% and driving about $0.02/share of upside. Net interest income (FTE basis) rose almost 9% YOY and more than 3% QOQ, driving three-quarters of that upside. Net interest margin improved to 3.07% (up 20bp YOY and 7bp QOQ), beating by 2bp, and earning assets were about as expected (up 1% YOY and QOQ).

Core fee-based core rose a little less than 8% YOY and fell about 2% QOQ, but that was still slightly better than expected. Capital markets was strong on an annual comparison (up 16%), but weaker sequentially (down about 16%) due in part to the government shutdown. Card fees were a bit light of expectations (down 11% YOY and 1% QOQ), and that’s a bit of a disappointment given recent launches.

Core opex rose 2% YOY and 1% QOQ, clawing back about $0.015/share of the revenue beat, though the efficiency ratio improved both annually and sequentially.

Pre-provision profits rose 21% YOY and 3% QOQ, beating by about half a cent versus sell-side expectations. The ratio of PPOP to AEA improved about 27bp, and while 1.6% isn’t superior, it’s trending in the right direction. A lower provision helped boost earnings, but was largely offset by a higher tax rate (vs. expectations) and a slightly higher share count.

Tangible book per share improved almost 18% YOY, while the CET 1 ratio came in slightly lower at 10.6%.

Reassuring Guidance Supports The Story

Citizens went into this quarterly reporting cycle with above-average earnings growth potential in 2026 (and 2027), and that remains the case, with pre-provision profits likely set for two years of mid-teens or better growth.

A Healthy Near-Term Outlook

Management guided to 10% to 12% net interest income growth, with ongoing improvement in net interest margin (around 5bp per quarter) being a major driver. Citizens is set to continue benefiting from loan and securities repricing, but also loan growth and the expiration/run-off of rate swaps, as well running off non-core loans. Underlying loan growth of 3% to 5% seems realistic, particularly with capital markets activity (M&A in particular) likely to help and ongoing growth in the Private Bank operations (which drove two-thirds of net loan growth this quarter).

Management also guided to 6% to 8% fee-based income growth, pointing in particular to strong deal pipelines for the capital markets business. Opex growth of 4.5% seems reasonable, with management reinvesting in growth drivers like private banking and private capital, as well as AI capabilities for customer service.

Looking at guidance, I see a few potential areas of risk worth monitoring.

Management’s guide for a cumulative interest-bearing deposit beta of 40% could be a bit optimistic given past cycles, though I think this rate cycle will be more supportive. There is also some macroeconomic risk here – particularly with respect to business loan demand. I do also see some credit risk, more on the consumer side, but this is not a Citizens-specific concern.

Improving Long-Term Opportunities

Citizens has been held back in the past at least in part by a weaker ROTCE relative to its peers (and there has long been a strong correlation between ROTCE and P/TBV multiples). This, in turn, has been an issue with multiple contributing causes, including suboptimal funding, a suboptimal loan mix, an overreliance on spread income, and a less efficient capital structure.

Almost all of this is changing for the better, and it should have a meaningful ongoing impact on underlying value in the years to come.

While Citizens has long had a sizeable middle-market lending franchise, the quality of its loans weren’t always the best, making the company (and stock) more vulnerable to credit cycles. That underlying quality has been improving, and management has likewise been building up its payments and capital markets capabilities to complement the lending franchise – offering a fuller suite of products to corporate customers, getting a bigger share of wallet with them, and generating strong incremental returns on that extra business.

In addition to running off less attractive loans, efforts to improve funding have borne some fruit, with a better loan/deposit ratio and improving cost of funds without sacrificing much on loan yields.

As far as the capital structure goes, this will take longer, but should be aided by a more transparent and less punitive regulatory environment. Citizens has had the highest stress capital buffer of its peer group (at 4.5%), and while its business structure means some of that will remain, I do expect an improvement here in the next few years, which will free up more capital for returns to shareholders.

Last and not least are the bank’s restructuring and growth initiatives. The “Reimagine the Bank” initiative could add two points to ROTCE over the next two years, with about two-thirds coming from cost efficiencies (including making greater use of AI in customer service). At the same time, the bank continues to invest in its private bank (which is already paying off in loan and deposit growth), while also targeting growth in the NYC metro area and payments businesses.

The Outlook

The next two or three years should see outsized growth for Citizens as the bank leverages past cost efficiency initiatives, a more capital-efficient balance sheet, better spreads, and growth investments like the private banking efforts. That, in turn, should drive double-digit core earnings growth over the next five years and well-above average 10-year growth, though core growth should slow to the 3%-4% range over time.

My 2026 EPS estimate is a bit above the average now (around 2%), as is my 2027 estimate (close to 5% above the average), though I expect the average will rise as more sell-side updates are published.

Between discounted core earnings, P/E, and ROTCE-driven P/TBV, I believe Citizens should trade closer to $70/share today. Core earnings support that figure, as does a 10.5x multiple on ’27 earnings and a 1.85x multiple on TBV based on a 15%-plus ROTCE in 2027. Clearly, valuation is based at least in part on a multi-year improvement cycle, as nearer-term metrics would reduce the fair value for P/E and P/TBV closer to $65.

The Bottom Line

The easy money is long gone here, as management has clearly delivered on self-improvement targets and put the bank on a footing that makes mid-to-high-teens ROTCEs much more credible now. That doesn’t mean that there’s no more growth or rerating to be had, but it does make the bank somewhat more sensitive to overall macro conditions. I still think CFG stock is a worthwhile buy/hold, particularly in a sector that is getting shorter on clearly undervalued or misunderstood stories, but it would certainly be a more interesting name on pullbacks.

data.title: Foreign Stocks Extend Lead Over U.S. Shares In 2026
data.description:
It was a vintage performance. President Trump skewered the edifice of globalization yesterday at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. Lest anyone miss the message, he claimed that “The United States is keeping the whole world afloat” and that the status quo would no longer endure.

US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick put a finer point on the President’s view, telling the Davos crowd: globalization is a “failed policy” that’s inflicted a high price on the US. “And what we are here to say is that ‘America First’ is a different model—one we encourage other countries to consider.”

Globalization may be on the defensive – some might say in its death throes — but from an investing perspective, it’s thriving. Global stocks are still outperforming US shares so far in 2026, extending last year’s winning run. Using a set of ETFs through yesterday’s close (Jan. 21), all the major equity regions of the world are maintaining a healthy lead over the US.

Topping the list by a wide margin so far this year: stocks in Latin America (ILF), which have surged more than 11% so far in 2026.

Global equities ex-US are ahead 4.2% year to date — that’s a solid lead over US stocks (SPY), which are still trailing the rest of the world via a thin 0.5% increase.

These are still early days for assessing the economic and investment implications of a US that’s redefining and reinventing its role on the world stage. But to the extent that markets are offering early clues on expectations, the results imply that the sentiment continues to favor international diversification. It would be ironic if, at some future point in time, analysts look back and identify Donald Trump as the catalyst that revived the fortune of global investing strategies. Presuming that will be the case is premature. But it’s somewhat more plausible at the moment, given the strong run in offshore stocks last year that’s spilling over into 2026.

A key question for the year ahead, and beyond: Is a secular shift unfolding in global markets? A pair of analysts at Breakout Capital, writing last week for the CFA Institute’s blog, advise that an attitude adjustment appears to be in progress.

We believe we are in a new regime where there will be an increased recognition that international markets are on the mend and offer strong earnings growth and policy improvement at much cheaper valuations.

The strong cyclical advantages that the US offered 15 years ago are increasingly being chipped away creating the conditions for a multi-year tailwind in favor of international markets.

That’s still a speculative view, albeit one that’s continued to resonate so far in 2026.

This much is clear: the old world order is breaking down. What replaces it is still up for grabs. Regime shift for markets and macro is always messy and full of surprises, along with ample opportunities. The average global investor suspects that the clues of what’s coming are beginning to emerge.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Proto Labs: Low Valuation Multiple With Signs Of Business Acceleration
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Proto Labs (PRLB) has pioneered the field of manufacturing-as-a-service. The firm helps its customers deliver on-demand manufacturing (avoiding overstocking or shortages on the other end), reduced lead time (rapid production and delivery), and, finally, design complexity (thanks to its 3D printing offering). The company serves more than 50k clients across different end markets through a complete portfolio of manufacturing machines: Injection Molding, CNC Machines, 3D Printing, and Sheet Metal. Company revenue trends have stalled in recent years, leading to a material valuation derating. However, recent quarters indicate some growth in constant currency. Using a positive mid-single-digit revenue CAGR in my DCF model with limited operating leverage, I see more upside potential. As a result, I am initiating coverage with a BUY rating.

Takeaways from the most recent conference calls

The recent Q2 and Q3 FY25 results indicated solid growth in the CNC segment. As a reminder, such machines use powerful tools that are operated by a computer. In Q2FY25, where the segment grew by +20% YoY, the management said :

In the U.S. alone, CNC machining revenue grew 30%. We continue to see very strong demand from Aerospace and Defense customers, specifically in high requirement parts. The value we deliver via our factory and network CNC machining offer is really resonating with our innovative customers.

Then, the CEO responded to a question by stating that CNC parts sold to customers are increasingly used for production, not just for prototyping.

In the Q3FY25 result, the management quoted interesting client names :

Our speed, precision, quality and reliability allow us to be a trusted partner to Amazon in several of its critical business units, notably drones and robotics. We have also supported Blue Origin with parts for their single-launch lunar cargo lander, among other projects. Blue Origin told us they continue to use Proto Labs' services due to our impeccable customer service and levels of detail and accountability.

Also, due to the strength of the CNC business, capacity expansion was highlighted, which can look like a bullish signal for future sales:

We are expanding CNC machining capacity in our factories, a significant signal of momentum and confidence. We expect this investment to generate a meaningful return.

Q3 results were once again pushed upward by this segment, growing by +17% YoY at constant currency.

Based on previous data, the following table shows that positive trends in the CNC segment began one year ago. This is encouraging, but it remains to be seen if other segments could participate in the recovery.

The management said it expects revenues to be between $125m and $133m, slightly below Q3's $135m. The firm can reach the upper side of this range as new capacity in the CNC business could fuel solid growth.

Margins were once again resilient thanks to cost optimization, which helped the firm beat EPS expectations.

Looking at cash flow generation, the last 9 months of FCF totaled $51m, nearly flat YoY. A higher revenue growth rate is required to generate more operating leverage.

The balance sheet is healthy, with a cash and equivalent position of $138m and no financial debt on the liability side. As FCF is positive, the financial risk is minimal.

Risks and opportunity

Even if the company's financials have materially improved over the recent quarters, helping the stock price to rebound, we are still below 2019 levels. It is a reminder to all that growth expectations have to be taken cautiously. Fortunately, priced at 2X EV/sales, we finally have some margin of safety against any growth disappointment.

On the opportunity side, new managers have recently joined the company. Suresh Krishna started as the CEO last May 2025, and Marc Kermichas is the new Chief Technology Officer with deep software knowledge. They are taking the lead in two key positions at a moment when growth seems to be accelerating, and a cost-cutting program has been in place. Being net cash and positive FCF, the firm could act as a market consolidator if needed.

What valuation can we expect?

I used the following hypotheses:

  • A gradual rebound in activity, starting in FY25 after a long pause for digestion, as recent quarterly results suggest stronger sales momentum.
  • No gross margin improvement, as higher utilization of production sites and cost optimization could be offset by price concessions to reach a larger addressable market.
  • A gradual operating leverage in the medium term, with long-term EBITDA margins reaching 20%.

With a terminal growth of 3% and a WACC of 9% accounting for low financial risk and its leading position, I get a target price of $69/share, indicating 30% upside potential.

Please find a sensitivity table on the stock.

Conclusion

With a modest company valuation, accelerating growth trends, and reasonably solid financials, the stock offers an attractive entry point. The new management should be able to deliver a new chapter for the company. I rate the stock as a BUY.

data.title: Disney: Q1 2026 May Bring Forth Outsized Performance - Reiterate Buy
data.description:
I previously covered Walt Disney Company (DIS) in September 2025, discussing how its well-diversified entertainment thesis across D2C/content licensing/theme parks/cruises had triggered its ongoing outperformance and the promising FY2025 guidance.

Combined with the reasonable valuations against historical trends/peers and the raised consensus estimates, I had believed that its investment thesis remained compelling then, resulting in my reiterated Buy rating upon a moderate dip.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the DIS stock here, thanks to the improved margin of safety from the cheap valuations/recent volatilities and the management's robust FY2026/FY2027 guidance, with it expected to trigger a robust upside potential while sustaining the excellent dividend growth/share retirement prospects.

This is significantly aided by the healthier balance sheet and the promising technical indicators, with the latter implying a potential breakout from the prior sideways trading since mid-2022.

DIS May Deliver Robust FQ1 2026 Numbers

Since my last article, DIS has indeed retraced near to my Buy zones of between $107s and $100s by November 2025 bottoms of $101s, before rebounding by +9% to the $110s by the time of writing.

Much of its recent recovery tailwinds are attributed to the market rotation after the AI-related meltdown in the Big Tech/AI sector to deep-value/dividend-oriented sectors in November 2025, with it benefiting DIS and some of its previously discounted streaming/TV/cruise peers in varying degrees.

I am of the opinion that DIS' recent recovery has been well-deserved indeed, since the management has offered robust FY2026 adj. EPS growth guidance by "double digit... compared to fiscal 2025" and promising preliminary FY2027 guidance by "double digit... compared to fiscal 2026," with these numbers building upon the excellent FY2025 adj. EPS performance at $5.93 (+19.3% YoY).

With DIS set to report their FQ1 2026 performance on February 02, 2026, I urge readers to closely monitor their performance against these metrics indeed.

For now, I am of the opinion that FQ1 2026 is likely to bring forth robust numbers for the Theatrical Distribution segment, thanks to the outperformance of Avatar: Fire and Ash, grossing $1.23B, and Zootopia 2, grossing over $1.65B globally by early January 2026.

This is despite lapping a tougher comparison from a year ago, attributed to Moana 2 at ~$1B and Mufasa: The Lion King at ~$700M, with it likely to well balance the management's FQ1 2026 guidance of lower theatrical operating income by -$400M and lower political advertising revenues by -$140M on a YoY basis.

I believe that DIS is likely to report robust D2C top-line and margin performance as well, based on the FQ1 2026 D2C SVOD operating income guidance of approximately $375M (+27.9% YoY).

This development may be attributed to the growing Disney+ subscriptions at 131.6M in FQ4 2025 (+7.2% YoY) and the expanding ARPU at $8.04 (+10.1% YoY), along with Hulu+ at 64.1M (+23.2% YoY) and $100.02 (+4.5% YoY), respectively, all of which are already contributing to the D2C segment's improved operating leverage at operating margins of 5.6% (+1.3 points YoY).

DIS is also taking a leaf out of Netflix's (NFLX) and Roku's (ROKU) playbooks through the planned gamification of its Disney+ platform in partnership with Epic Games and the planned integration of commerce capabilities into its D2C platform, respectively, allowing the former to increasingly enrich their user experience while expanding their monetization opportunities:

CTV will evolve into a true commerce engine – a space where entertainment, discovery and transaction happen seamlessly. As brands integrate their first-party data we’ll see more personalised, measurable campaigns that link exposure to outcome in real time... The biggest screen in the home will become more than a brand awareness engine; it will drive response by connecting creative storytelling directly to consumer behaviour. (Advanced Television)

At the same time, given the numerous holiday events in Q4 2025, including Thanksgiving and Christmas, I am of the opinion that DIS may report an outsized Experience performance, as hinted by the management, thanks to the robust "combination of some pricing and some attendance growth."

This is especially since Comcast's (CMCSA) recent Epic Universe opening has seemingly yet to make a dent in DIS' domestic park performance, with it signaling the latter's IP and demand resilience thus far:

We've talked about Epic in the past, in particular, as something that we knew was going to be a factor in domestic parks and, in fact, was very much in line with our expectations. If anything, it seems to be, in fact, impacting the rest of the competition down in Florida, more than it's impacting us. From a consumer perspective, we certainly feel good about it. (Seeking Alpha)

This is on top of the accretive impact of further expansion in its cruise fleet by two ships, Disney Destiny and Disney Adventure, in 2026, which is expected to "be a meaningful contributor to growth of Experiences during the course of the year, particularly in the second half as we get past the launch costs and some of the dry docks that we have in the first half of the year."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result of the robust performance metrics thus far and the excellent forward guidance, it is unsurprising that the consensus forward estimates appear to be rather promising, with DIS expected to report a robust bottom-line growth at a 3Y CAGR of +11.3% through FY2028.

This is despite the notable deceleration from the 5Y historical growth profile at +24% (between FY2020 and FY2025), albeit expanded compared to the 10Y growth at +1.4% (between FY2015 and FY2025).

These numbers underscore why DIS' recent stock price volatility has been unwarranted indeed, albeit with it triggering a great dip buying opportunity for those who have been patient.

DIS' Profitable Growth Is Cheaply Valued

Thanks to the recent volatility and the raised consensus forward estimates, it goes without saying that DIS is rather cheap at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 16.84x as well, down from the 1Y mean of 18.69x, the 5Y mean of 32.73x, and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 18.16x.

The same may be observed in my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.49x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 16.84x and the consensus adj. EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +11.3%, compared to the sector median of 1.20x, the 5Y mean of 1.92x, and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 3.02x.

A similar conclusion may also be derived after comparing DIS at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 16.84x and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.49x to its streaming/cruise peers, including:

  • Alphabet (GOOG) at 31.30x/1.78x,
  • NFLX at 34.71x/1.37x,
  • ROKU at 315.78x/1.77x,
  • Amazon (AMZN) at 33.81x/1.68x, and
  • Paramount Skydance (PSKY) at 17.82x/0.58x,

along with

  • Carnival Corporation & plc (CCL) at 11.35x/0.90x,
  • Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) at 17.64x/0.88x,
  • Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd. (NCLH) at 10.50x/0.58x, and
  • Viking Holdings (VIK) at 27.79x/0.93x, respectively,

with it underscoring DIS' still compelling valuations at current levels, thanks to the profitable, well-diversified entertainment offerings, the stock's recent volatility, and the promising FY2026/FY2027 guidance.

DIS Remains A Great Buy Here

For now, DIS has mostly traded sideways over the past three years, between the established support levels of $80s and the resistance levels of $124s, albeit with the stock seemingly well positioned to break out moving forward, as observed in the recent bounce off the intermediate support level of $100s in November 2025.

A similar conclusion may also be observed in DIS's technical indicators across the reasonable RSIs and the oversold trading volumes, with the stock likely to deliver robust capital appreciation moving forward.

Based on the FY2025 adj. EPS performance at $5.93 (+19.3% YoY) and the 5Y pre-pandemic P/E mean of 18.16x (cheap against its peer group P/E mean of ~22x, after discounting ROKU), it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to my fair value estimates of $107.60.

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj. EPS estimates of $8.19, there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +33.7% to my long-term price target of $148.70 as well.

While minimal, DIS' annualized dividend payout of $1.50 per share at expanded forward dividend yields of 1.35% (compared to the 2Y averages of 0.72%) already offers investors the chance to regularly add new shares through the DRIP program.

This is on top of the -0.7% shares retired over the LTM and -1.5% since FY2023, as the management also guides "doubling share repurchases target to $7 billion compared to fiscal 2025," with it underscoring the management's excellent capital allocation beyond the healthier balance sheet at net debts of $36.33B (-8.7% YoY/-12.7% from FY2019 levels of $41.65B).

As a result of the robust capital appreciation prospects arising from the double digits adj. EPS growth prospects through FY2027 and the cheap valuations, the excellent shareholder returns arising from dividend incomes and share repurchases, the healthier balance sheet, along with the promising technical indicators, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the DIS stock here.

data.title: Tesla: Underperformance For Our Top Short
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) continues to hover near all-time highs with a $1.4 trillion market cap. Still, the company, our top short recommendation of 2025, returned only 2% for the year versus 16% for the S&P 500 (SP500). While the company's share price has stayed higher for longer, we still see it as having massive downside, continuing it as our top short pick for 2026.

Tesla Q4 Delivery Results

Tesla has mediocre results in Q4, with 434K vehicles produced and 418K delivered.

The "other models" category of less than 12K vehicles shows how the Cybertruck has failed to garner any real traction and includes other valuable vehicles like the S and the X. For the year, the company's less than 1.64 million deliveries are down almost 10% YoY, yet this declining business makes up the majority of the company.

Tesla Shrinking YoY

Tesla's reported results come at 1.63 million vehicle deliveries for 2025, knocking down a TTM vehicle delivery number that was just over 1.7 million.

In fact, since peaking at the end of 2023 at just over 1.8 million vehicles in TTM deliveries, Tesla has been on a slow downtrend since. That's lined up with declining adjusted EBITDA and net income for the company, although the company has managed to keep FCF reasonable near 2022 peaks that were originally seen.

BYD (BYDDF) has officially overtaken Tesla as the largest EV company globally, and Tesla is moving to a regular growth company instead of a hyper-growth company. As a company that sells less than 2 million semi-luxury vehicles/year with no source to grow that, as its primary business, we see it as worth nowhere near $1.4 trillion.

On top of this, we see the source of new growth shrinking. The Cybertruck that once was going to sell millions has seen its battery contractor slash the contract value from billions of dollars to basically $0. The product, for lack of a better word, appears to be dead on arrival when it comes to moving the financial needle for Tesla.

Tesla FSD Subscription Only

Tesla has also shifted to FSD subscription only. The $8000 prior purchase is moving to $99/month.

One might say this is great for subscription revenue. However, the payback is now 7 years, and there's no guarantee that users will continue to hold on for 7-years without substantial improvement. Presuming 100% of users keep their subscription for 100% of the time, Tesla is earning a 15% return ($1200/year instead of $8000 upfront), however, if a portion of users don't, this could hurt the company substantially.

The flip side is the new pricing, even in the optimal cases, isn't a source of substantial additional cash. Keep in mind at one point FSD was $15K/$199/mo, which the company was forced to reduce, showing an inability to maintain strong pricing.

Tesla Optimus

Optimus, known as Tesla Bot, is a general purpose humanoid robot made by Tesla. The first prototype was shown in 2022, and since then, the bot has remained under development. Elon Musk has hyped this business up as the next big thing for Tesla, but AI experts remain uncertain on the actual potential here for growth and returns.

Another factor here is CEO Elon Musk's AI ambitions. Elon Musk has previously stated that he does not feel his equity in Tesla is large enough for him to pursue AI without a larger stake. Now it comes out that xAI is the company that will be developing the "AI brain" for the Optimus robot, an AI venture that Elon Musk has a much larger stake in.

That's not surprising, given prior employee moves from Tesla to xAI. As a result, despite Elon Musk's claims, the shift of the story of the value driver changing from self-driving to Optimus leads us to question whether Tesla will ever see any real money from Optimus.

Tesla Valuation

Putting together a valuation for Tesla:

Business P/E Total Value
Vehicle Production 10x earnings $50.0 billion
Vehicle Services 10x earnings $7.5 billion
Energy Storage 5x revenue $70.0 billion
Optimus N/A $40 billion
FSD N/A $55 billion
$222.5 billion

Putting together a valuation for Tesla, we're valuing the company's vehicle division at a P/E of 10x based on the high side of other car companies. The company's energy storage business is the most valuable at 5x revenue based on its rapid growth, even though we see competition increasing. This is a very lofty valuation not comparable to peers, but it's the only growing segment.

Then we get to the niche pieces. Optimus. We value Optimus at $40 billion based on the last capital raise of pure-play competitor Figure AI. Rumors are that Figure AI is passing Optimus. Then we hit full self-driving, or FSD. FSD is valued at half of Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Waymo, which is trying to raise a $110 billion round, based on FSD's lack of unsupervised driving without a safety driver.

Waymo is at almost 2 million paid rides a week.

The sum of the parts here is $222 billion, 16% of Tesla's present-day valuation. That shows how overvalued Tesla is and why, in our view, Tesla is our top short recommendation.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is Tesla's valuation and trading. Tesla trades more on news announcements and new projects (i.e., Optimus) than it does on the fundamentals of vehicle sales and earnings. The potential of a combination with another Musk company, i.e., xAI, could be a boon to the share price that makes holding a short very unprofitable.

Conclusion

Tesla underperformed the market substantially in 2025 but that's more due to a market that went up 16% versus Tesla only going up 3%. That's a double-digit underperformance, but if you were shorting the stock versus investing in the S&P 500 you wouldn't have been happy about the performance of your portfolio.

Shorting is fundamentally not just a bet against a company; it's a timed bet against a company, with risks. Despite that, we see major risks this year with BYD taking over along with our expectation for late delivery on FSD and Optimus. That makes Tesla a poor investment that we expect to underperform. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FCG: Strong Natural Gas Prices And Demand Supports The Uptrend In 2026
data.description:
A sharp rally in the U.S. natural gas prices (NG1:COM) helped First Trust Natural Gas ETF (FCG) to kick off 2026 on a stronger note. The uptrend is driven by the extremely cold weather conditions in the United States. Besides a weather-related uptrend, the market fundamentals suggest that the natural gas-focused ETFs, such as FCG, are expected to generate steady growth in 2026. Therefore, I initiate coverage of FCG with a Buy rating.

U.S. Natural Gas Prices Outlook

The U.S. natural gas futures are experiencing one of the fastest surge in the last three decades amid the freezing cold weather conditions across the country. Year to date, the U.S. natural gas futures are up 35%, extending twelve months gain to 45%.

Besides a weather-related surge, the natural gas fundamentals for mid and long term are likely to be strong, given the increasing use of natural gas to meet power demand and rising liquified natural gas exports. According to the EIA, the gas demand will outpace production growth in 2026 and ahead. Consequently, storage inventories are expected to decline below their rolling five-year average.

The U.S. LNG exports, which increased significantly after Russian invasion of Ukraine, are likely to be the largest contributor to natural gas demand growth in 2026 and ahead. In 2025, LNG exports increased by 24% with expectations for a double digit percentage boost in 2026 and 2027. The U.S. is likely to ramp-up its LNG production capacity by 20 mmt in 2026 to capitalize on growing demand. Last year, the country exported a record 111 mmt of LNG, making it the world’s largest exporter.

On the other hand, the electric utilities are investing hundreds of billions of dollars to increase their electric generation capacity to capitalize on rising demand from data centers. According to the S&P Global, the power demand from data centers could triple by the end of 2030 from an estimated 61.8 GW in 2025. The natural gas is the largest source for U.S. electricity generation, represented almost 43% of total generation in 2024.

Why FCG Is A Solid Option to Capitalize On The Potential Natural Gas Upside

There are numerous investment vehicles available in the market to capitalize on the potential upside in the natural gas market. For example, investors can use a single-stock investment strategy. However, a single-stock strategy carries a higher risk factor with a limited potential to fully capitalize on broader market trends.

Whereas, the exchange traded funds could be an interesting investment vehicles, given their potential to limit a risk factor while capitalizing on the broader market trends. The First Trust Natural Gas ETF appears to be a worth considering investment option, given its diversified portfolio, higher liquidity and low per share price. In addition, FCG offers a healthy dividend yield of 2.8%.

With a single investment, FCG enables investors to access 41 U.S. companies with exposure to natural gas exploration & production, transportation, storage and related services. Its portfolio also include MLPs. The fund seeks to track the performance of ISE-Revere Natural Gas Index by using full replication technique.

ConocoPhillips (COP), Western Midstream Partners LP (WES), Hess Midstream LP (HESM), Occidental Petroleum (OXY) and EOG Resources (EOG) are its top 5 holdings.

ConocoPhillips is an exploration & production company. Its Henry Hub-linked U.S. natural gas production capacity is more than 2 Bcf a day or 15 MTPA equivalent. In addition, the company’s exposure to Lower 48 and international assets can boost its gas production potential.

Western Midstream Partners, its second largest holding, is engaged in midstream activities, including the gathering, compressing, treating, processing, and transporting of natural gas. In the latest reported quarter, its natural-gas throughput was around 5.4 Bcf/d, representing a sequential increase of 2%. Hess Midstream LP is its third largest holdings. A U.S.-based MLP is engaged in operating, developing, and acquiring midstream assets while offering fee-based services. In the latest quarter, its gas processing throughput volumes increased 10%.

Occidental Petroleum, an integrated oil and gas company, is its fourth largest holding. The company has significant gas assets along with the midstream and marketing operations. Occidental Petroleum rely heavily on its assets in Permian Basin, Rockies Mountains and CrownRock for oil and gas production. Whereas, EOG Resources is an exploration & production company. In the latest reported quarter, EOG’s natural gas production was around 2,745 MMcfd while its NGL production was nearly 309.3 MBbld. EOG Resources has a multi–basin portfolio with the key assets located in Delaware Basin, Eagle Ford, and Utica.

Similarly, the fund’s other top 5 holdings also have the strong foot-holds in natural gas market. These stocks include, Diamondback Energy (FANG), Devon Energy Corp (DVN), APA Corp (APA), Permian Resources (PR) and Coterra Energy (CTRA). Overall, instead of investing in a single stock, a strategy of accessing an entire gas market through a single investment could enable investors to make the most out of the potential uptrend while limiting a risk factor.

FCG’s Other Key Characteristics

FCG’s other key characteristics include a solid dividend yield of nearly 2.83%. The majority of its holdings offer healthy dividends. Moreover, the fund’s exposure to high-yielding MLP’s increases its potential to pay dividends. FCG has paid dividends in the last 18 consecutive years. In the last five years, its dividend growth rate (CAGR) was around 14%.

In addition, FCG trades at a low per share price of $24. A low per share price increases the affordability factor. It also enables investors to buy shares in a large quantity. The fund’s liquidity factor is also strong with more than 859K in average shares trading volume compared to the median of all ETFs of 37K. Its expense ratio of 0.57% is slightly higher from the median of all ETFs of 0.50%.

Risk Factors to Consider

The energy sector is one of the most volatile sectors in the S&P 500 index (SP500). The natural gas prices are correlated to geopolitical and weather conditions. The demand and supply also plays a key role in determining gas prices. FCG’s standard deviation of 22 and annualized volatility of 32% is more than double compared to the median of all ETFs. Overall, the energy sector and natural gas market carries an aggressive risk factor.

In Conclusion

After years of sluggish growth, the natural gas market indicates a solid uptrend ahead. The natural gas demand is expected to exceed supply as the United States seeks to boost its LNG exports to new record levels in 2026 and ahead. Moreover, a higher electricity consumption is also likely to enhance natural gas demand in the years ahead. Therefore, it is one of the best times to invest in natural gas market through investment vehicles, such as First Trust Natural Gas ETF.

data.title: Klarna Proves Itself A BNPL/Neobank Disruptor - Initiate Buy With Caveats
data.description:
I previously covered Affirm (AFRM) and SoFi Technologies (SOFI) in November 2025, discussing the former's robust tailwinds arising from the increased BNPL adoption by numerous merchants/Big Techs/users and the latter's robust fintech/neobank growth prospects arising from the improved user acquisition/cross-selling trends.

In this article, I shall discuss Klarna (KLAR) and share my findings about the stock, continuing the theme surrounding the BNPL/fintech/neobank sector.

Given the discounted valuations, the high-growth prospects, the well-diversified BNPL/neobank capabilities, and the potential reversal to adj. EPS profitability: I am initiating a Buy rating for KLAR upon the materialization of a double-bottom technical indicator.

KLAR's BNPL/Neobank Prospects Discussed

For the uninitiated, KLAR is "a global digital bank and flexible payments provider" that operates "across 26 countries" while boasting "more than 850,000 retailers" as partners and numerous Payment Service Provider [PSP]/BigTech players, including Worldpay, Stripe (STRIP), Google Pay (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Apple Pay (AAPL), among others.

KLAR's global operations are also why the fintech company reports "over 114 million active users and 3.4 million transactions per day," with it trumping another BNPL player operating largely in the UCAN region, Affirm (AFRM), at 24.1M active users as of Q3'25 (FQ1'26).

At the same time, readers must note that KLAR boasts "a full banking license from Finansinspektionen, the Swedish Financial Supervisory Authority" since 2017, allowing them to offer select banking services in numerous EU countries.

This is on top of an electronic money institution license in the UK since July 2025 and a strategic "partnership with WebBank, a small financial institution based in Salt Lake City, Utah," allowing them to offer select banking services in the UK and the US as well.

These are important developments indeed, with it signaling KLAR's robust global BNPL/neo bank prospects at a time of cheaper borrowing costs, as similarly observed in the FQ4'25 guidance with:

  • Gross Merchandise Value of $38B at the midpoint (+16.2% QoQ/NA YoY),
  • revenues of $1.07B at the midpoint (+18.8% QoQ/+37.1% YoY, Historical Financial Statements), and
  • Transaction Margin Dollar of $395M at the midpoint (+40.5% QoQ/+24.6% YoY),

with it building upon the robust:

  • FY2024 growth profile at NA YoY, +23.7% YoY, and +12.1% YoY, and
  • FY2023 growth profile at NA YoY, +19.4% YoY, and +57.9% YoY, respectively.

Much of KLAR's success is attributed to the fee-related transaction and service revenues at $634M (+23.8% YoY), with it underscoring the success of its global platform as more merchants/PSPs adopt its BNPL offerings and its GMV consequently grows.

While interest income "derived from the banking activities of the Group is based on the geographic location of the financial institution for which financial instruments have been purchased" remains a smaller portion of its overall revenues at 29.7%, despite the robust +54.5% YoY growth profile, I am of the opinion that its growth prospects across BNPL and neobank capabilities are likely to be well balanced moving forward.

This is thanks to KLAR intensifying their cross-selling fintech/neobank efforts through:

  • shopping deals/loyalty perks/cashback,
  • mobile phone plans,
  • stock trading,
  • remittance payments,
  • Klarna debit cards with credit card benefits across savings accounts at higher APYs, numerous subscriptions, travel and purchase protection, and exclusive deals, along with
  • The future plans of "an all-encompassing financial super app that’s personalized and can offer non-financial services."

If anything, readers must note that the management has reported excellent adoptions for its Klarna Card at "over four million signups, already 15% of all global transactions" as a percentage of total transactions in October 2025, along with the accelerating Klarna Card GMV growth from the +51% YoY observed in FQ2'25 to +92% YoY in FQ3'25.

These metrics signal its robust potential to disrupt the intensely competitive debit card market within the US, despite the paid membership approach across different benefit tiers.

For now, KLAR remains unprofitable on an adj. EBITDA basis at FQ3'25 margins at -4.3% (-18.4 points YoY) and adj. EPS basis at -$0.25 (-600% YoY), with the YoY underperformance attributed to the planned accounting lag from the fair financing program on its bottom lines:

Per accounting rules Klarna books provisions for credit losses upfront while revenues are booked over the life of the loan. Therefore, we see near-term negative impact to transaction margin % as we scale financing volumes. These volumes will however be accretive to absolute US Transaction Margin Dollars in the medium-term. (Klarna)

If anything, with only 18% of KLAR's merchants currently offering the fair financing program, I urge readers to temper their expectations heading into the upcoming FQ4'25 earnings call sometime in early 2026.

This is because the management has highlighted their ambition to achieve a 100% adoption rate across its merchant base, with the ongoing ramp-up likely to trigger near-term noise in its bottom-line performance.

Otherwise, the current consensus forward estimates appear very promising indeed, with KLAR expected to report an outsized top-line growth at a 3Y CAGR of +24.7% between the FY2024 revenues of $2.81B and the consensus FY2027 revenue estimates of $5.46B.

This is on top of the consensus FY2026 adj. EPS estimates of $0.61 (+208.9% YoY), with it implying its reversal to profitability after numerous years of lumpy performances - with the recent meltdown by over -50% from the IPO levels seemingly sentiment-driven, instead of fundamentals.

KLAR Is Initiated as a Buy With a Caveat

For now, KLAR continues to chase new lows, with it remaining to be seen when the bulls may defend a floor after losing over half of its value post-IPO.

The meltdown is also why KLAR appears extremely compelling at FWD Price/Sales valuations of 2.94x and my estimated FY2027 valuations of 1.88x, based on the current Market Capitalization of $10.28B and the consensus FY2027 revenue estimates of $5.46B.

A similar conclusion may also be observed when comparing it to its BNPL/fintech/neobank peers, including:

  • BNPL - AFRM at FWD Price/Sales valuations of 5.78x and my estimated FY2027 valuations of 4.63x,
  • Neo bank/investment - SOFI at 9.06x/6x,
  • AI lending platform - Upstart (UPST) at 4.19x/2.89x,
  • neo bank - LendingClub Corporation (LC) at 2.31x/1.67x, and
  • PSP/P2P/BNPL - PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) at 1.55x/1.37x, respectively,

With it apparent that KLAR's high-growth cadence has been overly discounted since the recent IPO on September 10, 2025, especially given the projected improvements in its bottom lines from FY2026 onwards, once the company supposedly achieves improved operating leverage.

As a result, I am cautiously initiating a Buy rating for the KLAR stock, albeit with the caveat that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer, attributed to the potential capital losses from the ongoing correction.

Despite its notably oversold technical indicators, it may be more prudent to wait for a double bottom to materialize first, since its upcoming reversal to adj. EPS profitability is likely to be lumpy over the next few quarters before stabilizing nearer the end of FY2026.

This is on top of the risks from the likely to be lumpy macroeconomic normalization despite the prior rate cuts in 2025, as "JPMorgan now sees zero rate cuts in 2026 — and a hike coming the following year," with it presenting headwinds to KLAR's future loan portfolio/delinquency performance after the mixed optics from the "rising costs from longer-term lending and credit provisions" in FQ3'25.

For now, I urge some patience in the meantime.
